hack.memory: LISP = Locator / ID Separation. EID = who (endpoint IP). RLOC = where (VTEP IP). Control Plane Node = the phonebook mapping EID→RLOC. Edge nodes register endpoints; remote edges query. Endpoint can roam without re-addressing.
hack.practice: Draw a three-node SDA: edge A, edge B, control plane node. Walk an endpoint from A to B on paper: registration updates at CP Node, future lookups return RLOC B, old RLOC A becomes stale. That's mobility without IP change.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 60. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 17. Exam: 'what protocol provides SD-Access control plane?' = LISP. Know EID vs RLOC and that Control Plane Nodes host the mapping database.

6.3.e.info:
Cisco TrustSec (CTS) with Scalable Group Tags (SGTs) is SD-Access's policy and segmentation engine. Instead of writing ACLs that say 'src 10.1.1.0/24 → dst 10.2.2.0/24', you tag users/devices by role and write policies between roles. This is called group-based or intent-based segmentation.

How SGTs work:

  1. An endpoint authenticates via 802.1X / MAB / WebAuth to Cisco ISE.
  2. ISE evaluates the user/device context (identity, posture, location) and returns an SGT — a 16-bit tag like 'Employee' (10), 'Guest' (20), 'IoT' (30), 'Contractor' (40).
  3. The edge node applies the SGT to all traffic from that endpoint — carrying it across the fabric in VXLAN's SGT field.
  4. At the egress (or any policy enforcement point), the device consults a Security Group ACL (SGACL) matrix: 'SGT 20 → SGT 30 = deny'. Traffic is permitted or dropped based on the tag pair, not IP addresses.

Why it's better than IP-based ACLs:

  • Independent of IP — policy survives re-addressing, DHCP changes, VM moves.
  • Scales to microsegmentation — one policy row per group pair, not per host pair.
  • Identity-aware — same user on a corporate laptop vs a personal phone can get different tags and policies.
  • Centrally managed — policies live on DNA Center / ISE, not scattered in per-device ACLs.

Exam expectation: know that SDA policy is SGT-based (not IP-based), that SGTs come from ISE after authentication, and that SGACLs define what tag pairs can communicate. You won't configure SGTs on the CCNA; recognizing the model is enough.

hack.memory: CTS + SGT = identity-based policy. User authenticates to ISE → ISE returns an SGT → fabric tags traffic → SGACL at egress allows/denies based on tag pairs. Policy independent of IP. This is microsegmentation done right.
hack.practice: Design a 4-tag policy matrix: Employee, Guest, IoT, Server. Fill in allow/deny between each pair. Realize the matrix has 16 cells, vs IP-based ACLs that would need hundreds of rules. That density advantage is why CTS exists.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 60. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 17. Exam: 'SD-Access policy is based on?' = SGT / group / TrustSec. Know the ISE → SGT → SGACL flow at a conceptual level.

6.4.a.info:
Generative AI creates new content — text, configs, code, diagrams — from natural-language input. In networking, it's the class of AI that lets an engineer type 'give me an ACL blocking HTTP from guest VLAN to server VLAN' and get back valid IOS config.

Common generative AI use cases in network ops:

  • Configuration drafting — generate VLAN, ACL, QoS, or BGP snippets from plain-language requirements.
  • Log and alert summarization — condense thousands of syslog lines into 'your core router had a BGP flap at 02:41'.
  • Troubleshooting assistants — walk through suspected root causes step by step when given symptoms.
  • Documentation and diagrams — produce runbooks, change records, topology maps from live state.
  • Code generation — Python scripts, Ansible playbooks, Postman collections for specific tasks.
  • Platform translation — convert IOS config to NX-OS, or CLI to YANG JSON.

Key constraint — hallucinations. Generative AI produces plausible-sounding output, which may or may not be correct. It can invent wildcard masks, reference commands that don't exist, or miss edge cases. Every AI-generated config must be reviewed, tested in a lab, and validated before being pushed to production. This is exam-testable: AI augments engineers, it doesn't replace validation.

Cisco context: Cisco AI Assistant (part of Catalyst Center and Security Cloud) is the vendor's generative offering. General-purpose tools — ChatGPT, Claude, GitHub Copilot — are heavily used across the industry for the same tasks.

hack.memory: Generative AI = CREATES output. In networking: configs, docs, scripts, troubleshooting steps from plain-English input. Always hallucinates occasionally — humans must validate. Cisco flavor = Cisco AI Assistant.
hack.practice: Ask a chat AI to write an IOS ACL for a specific policy. Compare its output to what you'd write by hand. Note at least one subtle mistake it makes — that's why validation is non-negotiable.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab (updated content). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 17 (newer printings). New CCNA 200-301 v1.1 topic. Exam: 'what does generative AI do?' = creates content from natural language input.

6.4.a.info:
The CCNA 200-301 v1.1 adds generative AI as a knowledge-level topic. You don't have to know how models work — you have to recognize what generative AI can and should do in a NOC or engineering context. Memorize the common use cases so you can identify them in a multiple-choice stem.

High-confidence use cases (safe to recognize on the exam):

  • Config generation from natural language — 'block telnet from guest VLAN' → ACL lines.
  • Log/alert summarization — hundreds of syslog messages compressed into a human-readable incident summary.
  • Runbook and documentation drafting — turn a change request into a procedure document.
  • Troubleshooting step-by-step suggestions — symptom-driven diagnostic walkthroughs.
  • Code generation — Python / Ansible for a specific repeatable task.
  • Explaining output — 'what does this show ip ospf database output tell me?'

Lower-confidence use cases (still valid but need more oversight):

  • Writing production-critical security policy from scratch.
  • Generating large, multi-device configuration changes end-to-end without human review.
  • Making runtime decisions (AIOps / predictive) — that's predictive AI's job, not generative AI.

Key contrast with predictive AI (6.4.b): generative creates; predictive forecasts. The exam may list both and ask which is which — if the answer talks about writing a config or summarizing a log, it's generative. If it talks about forecasting a failure or detecting anomalies, it's predictive.

Career framing: in NOC and DC-ops work, generative AI is already part of the job — writing playbooks, summarizing tickets, drafting change docs. Knowing how to use it responsibly (validate everything) is the real skill.

hack.memory: Gen-AI creates text/config/code from natural language. Top 6 use cases: configs, summaries, runbooks, troubleshooting steps, scripts, explain-this-output. Always validate — models hallucinate. Gen = creates; predictive = forecasts.
hack.practice: Match these to 'generative' or 'predictive': summarize 500 syslogs (gen), detect hardware failure before it happens (pred), write an ACL from a sentence (gen), flag unusual traffic at 3 AM (pred). Build the instinct.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab (updated content). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 17. Exam: 'which of these is a generative AI use case?' — pick the one about CREATING output from natural language.

6.4.b.info:
Predictive AI uses machine-learning models trained on historical network data to forecast future events. Where generative AI creates content, predictive AI spots trends, flags anomalies, and predicts failures before they happen.

Core capabilities:

  • Anomaly detection — baseline normal behavior, alert when today deviates. Catches zero-day malware, brute force attempts, and misconfigurations that static rules miss.
  • Failure prediction — optical transceivers, fans, PSUs, and storage all emit early warning signs (rising CRC, temperature drift, SMART counters) before they fail. ML models learn these patterns and raise flags.
  • Capacity forecasting — trends in link utilization, CPU, and memory are extrapolated into 'link X will hit 80% in 6 weeks'. Enables planned upgrades instead of emergency ones.
  • User-experience prediction — predict that a given Wi-Fi client will have poor voice-call quality based on RSSI, retries, and neighboring APs; preemptively roam the client or adjust the radio.

Data fuel: predictive AI needs historical data — NetFlow, streaming telemetry, syslog, SNMP counters, wireless RRM data, device inventory. The more structured, long-lived telemetry you have, the better the models.

Why it matters operationally: predictive AI shifts operations from reactive (fix after it breaks) to proactive (fix before it breaks). Paired with self-healing automation (6.1.e), this can almost eliminate certain classes of outages from the incident log.

Cisco examples: Catalyst Center Assurance, Cisco AI Endpoint Analytics, Meraki AI-driven wireless optimization, ThousandEyes AI Insights for Internet/cloud path analytics.

hack.memory: Predictive AI = FORECASTS future events. Top uses: anomaly detection, failure prediction, capacity forecasting, user-experience prediction. Shifts ops from reactive → proactive. Cisco's flavor = Catalyst Center Assurance.
hack.practice: For each example, identify what historical data would feed the model: CRC errors → optical failure prediction. BW utilization over 6 months → capacity forecast. Wi-Fi retries + RSSI → client experience. Data → model → prediction.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab (updated content). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 17. Exam: 'predictive AI does what?' = forecasts events / detects anomalies / predicts failures. Goal: proactive ops.

6.4.b.info:
The CCNA tests the distinction between generative and predictive AI directly. Get the contrast clean and you unlock every 6.4 question.

DimensionGenerative AIPredictive AI
Primary outputNew content (text, config, code)Forecasts, scores, classifications
InputNatural language promptStructured telemetry / historical data
Model classLLMs, diffusion modelsClassifiers, regressors, time-series ML
ExamplesWrite an ACL, summarize logs, generate a runbookAnomaly detection, failure prediction, capacity planning
RiskHallucinations — fabricated but plausible outputFalse positives, model drift, bias
GuardrailHuman validation of every output before deployCalibrated thresholds + human review of high-impact actions

Shortcut: if the exam's verb is 'create / write / draft / generate / summarize', the answer is generative. If it's 'forecast / predict / detect anomaly / baseline', the answer is predictive. They aren't opposites — they're complementary tools addressing different problems.

In real ops: they layer together. Predictive AI spots an anomaly; generative AI then drafts the troubleshooting runbook or the fix script. Both operate under human supervision in mature teams.

Cisco split: Catalyst Center Assurance is mostly predictive (AIOps, anomaly detection). Cisco AI Assistant is generative (natural-language config, query, summarize). Same platform, two different AI classes.

hack.memory: Gen = CREATE. Predictive = FORECAST. Verb test: 'write / summarize / draft' → gen. 'Predict / detect / baseline' → predictive. Both are real, both layer together, both need humans. Cisco: Assistant (gen), Assurance (predictive).
hack.practice: Rapid-fire 10 mini scenarios: 'draft a BGP config from English' (gen), 'predict next month's link saturation' (pred), 'summarize last night's syslog' (gen), 'detect anomalous DNS traffic' (pred). Repeat until instant.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab (updated content). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 17. Exam: 'which AI class does X?' — gen creates, pred forecasts. This is the single most-tested distinction in 6.4.

6.4.b.info:
Anomaly detection is predictive AI's marquee use case. ML models learn what 'normal' looks like for a given metric, device, or user and raise alerts when observed behavior deviates from that baseline in a statistically significant way.

Why anomaly detection beats static thresholds:

  • Context aware — 90% CPU at 9 AM Monday might be normal; 90% at 3 AM Sunday is almost certainly a problem. Static 'CPU > 80' alerts can't tell the difference.
  • Slow drift — metrics that creep up over weeks never trip a static threshold until the day they do. ML spots the trend line long before impact.
  • Multivariate — a single metric may look fine, but ML can correlate 'high CPU AND low throughput AND high retransmits' to flag the pattern a human would miss.

Common security anomaly-detection targets:

  • DNS tunneling — unusual query volumes or uncommon record types.
  • Data exfiltration — outbound flows from an endpoint suddenly 10x normal.
  • Lateral movement — one host suddenly probing many internal hosts on unusual ports.
  • Compromised credentials — a user logging in from two continents within an hour.

Common infrastructure anomaly-detection targets:

  • Interface errors rising on a specific port (failing transceiver).
  • Wireless client drop rate spiking on one AP (environmental interference).
  • Storage read latency climbing on one array (aging disk).

Trade-offs: ML baselines can also miss anomalies that mimic normal patterns (evasion techniques), and produce false positives when legitimate behavior changes (marketing campaign traffic spike). Mature deployments combine ML anomaly detection with human-in-the-loop review and explicit allow-lists.

hack.memory: Anomaly detection = 'what is normal?' → 'is now abnormal?' Beats static thresholds because it's context-aware and catches slow drift. Security catches (DNS tunneling, exfil, lateral movement) + infra catches (optic failures, AP issues, storage latency).
hack.practice: For one metric (interface CRC errors), sketch a baseline (near zero normally) and a 10-day rising trend. Static threshold at '>1000/min' triggers too late. ML picks up the upward slope on day 3. That's the value.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab (updated content). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 17. Exam: 'what's anomaly detection?' = ML-based identification of deviations from learned normal patterns. Used in both security and infra ops.

6.4.c.info:
ML-based baselines are the engine behind modern anomaly detection. Instead of an operator typing 'CPU > 80%' into a monitoring tool, the ML model learns what CPU (and hundreds of other metrics) look like normally for each device, at each time, in each context — then uses those learned patterns as the threshold.

What the model actually learns:

  • Time-of-day and day-of-week patterns — Monday 9 AM ≠ Sunday 3 AM.
  • Seasonality — end-of-quarter spikes, holiday lulls, school-year patterns.
  • Per-device baselines — a data-hall spine switch vs a branch office router have very different normals.
  • Per-application baselines — VoIP, video, backup, and SaaS each have their own traffic signatures.
  • Multivariate baselines — relationships between metrics (CPU + throughput + retransmits) as a set.

Static thresholds vs ML baselines — head to head:

ScenarioStaticML baseline
85% CPU at 9 AM MondayAlerts (false positive)Normal, no alert
85% CPU at 3 AM SundayAlerts (correct)Alert — highly abnormal
CPU creeping 50→75% over 6 monthsSilentTrend alert weeks in advance
60% CPU but throughput dropped 40%SilentMultivariate anomaly alert

Operational impact: ML baselines drastically reduce alert fatigue (fewer false positives during normal busy periods) and catch classes of problems (slow drift, multivariate issues) that humans miss entirely.

Where they live: Catalyst Center Assurance, Meraki AI, ThousandEyes, Splunk ITSI, Dynatrace. All use the same underlying idea — learn what normal looks like, alert on deviation.

hack.memory: ML baseline LEARNS normal — per device, per time, per variable combo. Static thresholds are dumb: one number all day. ML alerts catch context (85% at 3 AM) and slow drift (50→75% over months). Alert fatigue drops; real issues rise.
hack.practice: Pick one metric on a lab device (interface utilization). Define a static threshold AND imagine the ML baseline learning hour-by-hour patterns over a week. Identify 2 scenarios where the static threshold is wrong and ML wins.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab (updated content). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 17. Exam: 'ML-based monitoring differs from static thresholds how?' = learns context-aware patterns + catches slow drift + multivariate.

6.4.c.info:
Cisco DNA / Catalyst Center Assurance is the concrete, CCNA-testable example of ML-driven monitoring and AIOps. It's one of the four DPPA pillars (6.2.d.2) and the most AI-heavy part of the controller.

What Assurance does, in practice:

  • Health scores — every device and every client gets a numeric health score (0-10) computed from dozens of underlying metrics. Green/yellow/red surfaces at the site, building, and floor level.
  • Issue correlation — an AP flapping, Radius authentication failing, and a client stuck in association all correlate into ONE issue: 'wireless clients cannot onboard to AP-21'. Hundreds of raw events → one actionable issue.
  • Root-cause analysis — ML models suggest the most likely cause. 'Radar interference on 5 GHz is triggering DFS channel changes, causing client disconnects.'
  • Guided remediation — step-by-step fix instructions surfaced directly in the UI, often with one-click actions that the controller can execute.
  • Path trace — visualize the L2/L3 path a packet takes between two endpoints and see per-hop health / policy in one view. No more SSH-ing into five switches.

Under the hood: Assurance ingests NetFlow, streaming telemetry (gNMI), SNMP, syslog, wireless RRM data, ISE context, and running configs. ML models build per-metric baselines, run anomaly detection, and cross-correlate events. Results surface as a prioritized issue list.

Why the CCNA tests it: it's the single best concrete example of predictive AI, ML baselines, and AIOps in Cisco's product line. Expect questions like 'which DNA Center pillar uses AI/ML?' (Assurance) or 'what provides root-cause analysis for wireless issues?' (Catalyst Center Assurance).

hack.memory: DNA / Catalyst Center Assurance = Cisco's ML / AIOps pillar. Health scores + issue correlation + root-cause analysis + guided remediation + path trace. Ingests NetFlow + telemetry + syslog + ISE context. Fourth DPPA pillar.
hack.practice: In the free DNA Center sandbox, open Assurance. Pick a site with issues; click through to root-cause analysis and guided remediation. Seeing it live makes the AIOps concept concrete in a way no diagram can.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 60. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 17. Exam: Assurance = the ML/AIOps arm of DNA / Catalyst Center. Pairs with DPPA and with 6.4.b predictive AI.

6.4.d.info:
AIOps (Artificial Intelligence for IT Operations) is the umbrella term for applying AI/ML to operational data — events, metrics, logs, traces — to automate the toughest parts of running infrastructure: correlation, root-cause analysis, and remediation.

AIOps loop (read this as one picture):

  1. Ingest — pull events from many sources (syslog, SNMP, streaming telemetry, ticketing, APM, NetFlow).
  2. Deduplicate + correlate — hundreds or thousands of raw events per incident → a single grouped incident.
  3. Root-cause — ML identifies the most likely origin ('uplink down on dist-sw-3').
  4. Recommend / act — suggest a runbook or execute an approved playbook automatically.
  5. Learn — feed back post-incident review so future correlations and recommendations improve.

Alert volume reduction is the big win. Without AIOps, a single major incident can produce 1,000+ alerts across monitoring tools — overwhelming on-call engineers and burying the real signal. With AIOps, those 1,000 alerts collapse into 1 incident with a rank-ordered list of likely causes. Operators spend time fixing, not triaging.

AIOps vs predictive AI vs generative AI:

  • Predictive AI — detects / forecasts (component of AIOps).
  • Generative AI — writes / summarizes (can augment AIOps with natural-language output).
  • AIOps — the operational umbrella that glues correlation + prediction + automation together.

Cisco example: DNA / Catalyst Center Assurance is an AIOps platform. Non-Cisco examples: Moogsoft, BigPanda, Dynatrace Davis, Splunk ITSI, New Relic AI. All solve the same correlation + root-cause + remediation problem.

hack.memory: AIOps = the AI loop for ops. Ingest → correlate → root-cause → act → learn. Main win: collapses 1,000 alerts into 1 incident. Includes predictive AI + automation + (sometimes) generative summaries. Cisco example = DNA / Catalyst Center Assurance.
hack.practice: Map a real NOC incident to the AIOps loop: 50 SNMP traps + 20 syslog + 5 tickets → dedupe/correlate → root-cause (core switch uplink) → runbook (failover) → post-incident review. Five steps, one picture.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab (updated content). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 17. Exam: 'AIOps does what?' = correlates events + automates root-cause analysis + drives remediation. Cisco concrete example = DNA Center Assurance.

6.4.d.info:
Event correlation is the process of grouping many low-level events into a smaller set of meaningful incidents. It's the engine that makes AIOps usable — without correlation, an operator drowns in raw alerts.

Correlation types:

  • Temporal — events arriving within a short window are likely related.
  • Topological — events from devices close together in the network graph are likely related (shared uplink).
  • Causal — known relationships like 'when this uplink fails, these downstream OSPF neighbors will drop'.
  • Pattern-based / ML — learned signatures of past incidents matched against current event streams.

Concrete example. A core switch power supply fails at 02:14. Within 30 seconds:

  • 40 OSPF adjacency-down syslog messages from neighboring routers.
  • 150 STP topology change events from access switches.
  • 300 interface-down SNMP traps.
  • 20 application timeouts from APM tools.
  • 12 user tickets from the help desk.

Without correlation, the on-call engineer sees 500+ alerts and has to piece together the story manually. With AIOps correlation, all 500+ events roll up into ONE incident: 'core-sw-A power failure at 02:14, impact: 200 downstream devices'. Triage goes from 30 minutes to 30 seconds.

Why correlation reduces MTTR more than raw alert routing: the biggest operational time sink isn't acting — it's figuring out which of a thousand alerts is the real one. Correlation removes that bottleneck so engineers jump straight to 'what do we do?' instead of 'what's actually broken?'

hack.memory: Event correlation = turn 1,000 alerts into 1 incident. Four modes: temporal (same time), topological (same region), causal (known dependency), ML (learned signature). Reduces triage time way more than 'faster acting' ever could.
hack.practice: Walk through a core-switch PSU failure scenario on paper: list every event it would produce across OSPF, STP, SNMP, APM, tickets. Count them. Now collapse into one incident. That collapse = correlation's ROI.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab (updated content). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 17. Exam: 'event correlation does what?' = groups related events into incidents. Key benefit = reduces alert noise / triage time.

6.4.d.info:
Automated root-cause analysis (RCA) is the step after correlation: once events are grouped, the AIOps engine tries to identify why the incident happened. It goes from 'something is wrong in this region' to 'uplink Gi1/0/24 on dist-sw-3 is flapping, causing the downstream OSPF adjacencies to drop, which is the cause of 47 correlated alerts'.

How the engine decides:

  • Topology awareness — the system knows which device depends on which. A failure at the top of the dependency graph is a more likely root cause than a downstream symptom.
  • Temporal ordering — the first event in a correlated group is often closer to the root cause than later ones.
  • Historical pattern match — 'this event mix is 90% similar to incident #12847 from 3 months ago, root cause was X'.
  • Change correlation — did someone push a config in the last hour? A recent change is a strong root-cause candidate.
  • Telemetry corroboration — counters (errors, drops, temperature) on the suspected device confirming the hypothesis.

Why 'automated' matters: humans can do root-cause analysis — but it takes training, time, and composure under pressure. A Level 1 NOC engineer at 3 AM trying to troubleshoot a fabric-wide outage is unlikely to arrive at the right cause quickly. Automated RCA surfaces a prioritized candidate list in seconds, letting humans focus on validating and fixing.

Limits and cautions: RCA is probabilistic. It can be confidently wrong, especially in rare or novel incidents. Mature operations treat automated RCA as a starting point, not a final verdict. Operators still validate before acting on high-risk remediations.

Cisco concrete example: Catalyst Center Assurance root-cause analysis for wireless issues is the most CCNA-relevant reference — it will name the failing AP, the contributing channel condition, and the impacted clients, with a confidence score.

hack.memory: Automated root-cause = engine picks the most likely WHY after correlation groups the WHAT. Uses topology + timing + history + change data + telemetry. Probabilistic — humans still validate before acting. Cisco concrete = Catalyst Center Assurance RCA.
hack.practice: Chain the three AIOps steps: 500 alerts → correlate into 1 incident → RCA points at dist-sw-3 uplink as likely cause → human confirms and runs failover playbook. End-to-end this flow on paper until it's second nature.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab (updated content). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 17. Exam: 'automated root cause analysis does what?' = identifies the most likely cause of a correlated incident, with confidence scoring. Cisco example = DNA Center Assurance.
